content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

September 29, 2011


Brian W. Jones
Assemblymember 77th District
State Capitol
P O Box 942849
Sacramento, CA 94249-0077

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-11-164

Dear Assemblymember Jones:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding honorarium provisions of the
Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

QUESTION

May you accept payments for making speeches as part of a business for which the
predominant activity is making speeches?

CONCLUSION

You may not accept payments for making speeches, when the predominant activity of the
business for which you make speeches is making speeches.

FACTS

You are an Assemblymember in the California Legislature. Prior to your election to the
Assembly, you were a paid staff member (associate pastor) for Sunrise Community Church in
Santee, California from 2001 to 2006. Your responsibilities included speaking to church groups
about spiritual matters, as well as current affairs. You were also vice-president of your own
commercial real estate company that dealt with church properties from 2006 to 2009. As part of
your responsibilities for your commercial real estate company, you spoke to church groups

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-11-164
Page No. 2

regarding development and church business issues. You have been compensated for speaking to
church groups about a variety of issues long before you became a member of the Assembly.

You would like to know if you may now receive an honorarium for speaking to religious
groups and gatherings. These speaking engagements will mostly deal with church issues.

ANALYSIS

Section 89502(a) provides that "no elected state officer, elected officer of a local
government agency, or other individual specified in Section 87200 shall accept any honorarium."
As a member of the Assembly you are an elected state officer, therefore you may not accept any
honorarium.

Section 89501 defines the term "honorarium," in pertinent part, as follows:

"(a) For purposes of this chapter, "honorarium" means, except as provided in
subdivision (b), any payment made in consideration for any speech given, article
published, or attendance at any public or private conference, convention, meeting,
social event, meal, or like gathering.

"(b) The term "honorarium" does not include:

"(1) Earned income for personal services which are customarily provided in
connection with the practice of a bona fide business, trade, or profession,
such as teaching, practicing law, medicine, insurance, real estate, banking, or
building contracting, unless the sole or predominant activity of the business, trade,
or profession is making speeches. The Commission shall adopt regulations to
implement this subdivision."

"(2) Any honorarium which is not used and, within 30 days after receipt, is either
returned to the donor or delivered to the State Controller for donation to the General
Fund, or in the case of a public official for local government agency, delivered to his
or her agency for donation to an equivalent fund, without being claimed as a
deduction from income for tax purposes.

Interpreting Section 89501(b)(1), Regulation 18932 provides:

"(a) 'Honorarium' does not include income earned from personal services if:

"(1) The services are provided in connection with an individual's business or the
individual's practice of or employment in a bona fide business, trade, or profession,
such as teaching, practicing law, medicine, insurance, real estate, banking, or
building contracting, pursuant to [Regulations 18932.1 through 18932.3]; and

File No. A-11-164
Page No. 3

"(2) The services are customarily provided in connection with the business, trade, or
profession." (Emphasis added.)

Regulation 18932.1(d) states that "a business whose predominant activity is making
speeches is not 'bona fide' for purposes of [Section 89501 through 89506]."

You are no longer employed as a pastor or as president of your commercial real estate
company. While you can give speeches in connection with some other "business" from which
you receive income, Regulation 18932.1(d) bars you from receiving payments for providing
services as a speaker for a business whose predominant activity is making speeches. Under your
facts, it appears that your "business" has as its sole or predominant activity making speeches.
Therefore, the payments you ask about fall directly within the prohibition of Section 89501
against receiving an honorarium and would be prohibited.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Zackery P. Morazzini
General Counsel

By: Sukhi K. Brar
Counsel, Legal Division

SKB:jgl

sections.question:
May you accept payments for making speeches as part of a business for which the 
predominant activity is making speeches?
sections.conclusion:
You may not accept payments for making speeches, when the predominant activity of the 
business for which you make speeches is making speeches.

You are an Assemblymember in the California Legislature. Prior to your election to the 
Assembly, you were a paid staff member (associate pastor) for Sonrise Community Church in 
Santee, California from 2001 to 2006. Your responsibilities included speaking to church groups 
about spiritual matters, as well as current affairs. You were also vice-president of your own

You would like to know if you may now receive an honorarium for speaking to religious

sections.analysis:
Section 89502(a) provides that "no elected state officer, elected officer of a local 
government agency, or other individual specified in Section 87200 shall accept any honorarium." 
As a member of the Assembly you are an elected state officer, therefore you may not accept any 
honorarium. 
Section 89501 defines the term "honorarium," in pertinent part, as follows: 
"(a) For purposes of this chapter, "honorarium" means, except as provided in 
subdivision (b ), any payment made in consideration for any speech given, article 
published, or attendance at any public or private conference, convention, meeting, 
social event, meal, or like gathering. 
"(b) The term "honorarium" does not include: 
"(1) Earned income for personal services which are customarily provided in 
connection with the practice of a bona fide business, trade, or profession, 
such as teaching, practicing law, medicine, insurance, real estate, banking, or 
building contracting, unless the sole or predominant activity of the business, trade, 
or profession is making speeches. The Commission shall.adopt regulations to 
implement this subdivision." 
"(2) Any honorarium which is not used and, within 30 days after receipt, is either 
returned to the donor or delivered to the State Controller for donation to the General 
Fund, or in the case of a public official for local government agency, delivered to his 
or her agency for donation to an equivalent fund, without being claimed as a 
deduction from income for tax purposes. 
Interpreting Section 89501(b)(l), Regulation 18932 provides: 
"(a) 'Honorarium' does not include income earned from personal services if: 
"(1) The services are provided in connection with an individual's business or the 
individual's practice of or employment in a bona fide business, trade, or profession, 
such as teaching, practicing law, medicine, insurance, real estate, banking, or 
building contracting, pursuant to [Regulations 18932.1 through 18932.3]; and  
"(2) The services are customarily provided in connection with the business, trade, or 
profession." (Emphasis added.) 
Regulation 18932.l(d) states that "a business whose predominant activity is making 
speeches is not 'bona fide' for purposes of [Section 89501 through 89506]." 
You are no longer employed as a pastor or as president of your commercial real estate 
company. While you can give speeches in connection with some other "business" from which 
you receive income, Regulation 18932.l(d) bars you from receiving payments for providing 
services as a speaker for a business whose predominant activity is making speeches. Under your 
facts, it appears that your "business" has as its sole or predominant activity making speeches. 
Therefore, the payments you ask about fall directly within the prohibition of Section 89501 
against receiving an honorarium and would be prohibited.
